timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Jeremiah Buys a Field

sections.header: Verses 1-13 -- "Call to Me and I Will Answer"

heb.paragraph: "Call to me and I will answer you and tell you great and unsearchable things (betsurот) you do not know" (v.3). The root btsr means "to fortify / make inaccessible." God promises to reveal things that are fortified against human discovery -- truths that cannot be reached by human effort but only by divine disclosure. This is revelation theology: truth comes from God, not from human investigation.

refs.note: "You will call upon me and come and pray to me, and I will listen" -- the same call-response dynamic from the letter to the exiles.
"If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God" -- the NT invitation to seek divine revelation through prayer.

notes.note: "Call to me" -- the invitation comes while Jeremiah is imprisoned. God's revelation is not limited by the prophet's circumstances. Prison walls do not block prayer.
Healing, peace, cleansing, pardon -- four dimensions of restoration. The city that was torn down will become God's "renown, joy, praise and honor before all nations."
The sounds of joy will return: the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom, the voice of those bringing thank offerings. What was silenced (7:34) will be restored.
Calvin calls this "the charter of prayer: God binds himself to answer. The promise is not conditional on the worthiness of the pray-er but on the faithfulness of the One who promises."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The word betsurот (often translated "unsearchable" or "hidden") literally means "fortified/inaccessible." The imagery is of truth locked in a fortress that only God can open.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom reads this as a "second consolation oracle" parallel to chapters 30-31. The same themes recur -- healing, joy, covenant -- but in compressed form.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies this as "the epistemological charter of prophetic faith." Knowledge of the future does not come from analysis or prediction but from prayer. The hidden things are revealed to those who call.

hist.context: Chapter 33 continues the consolation while Jeremiah is still confined in the courtyard of the guard. The famous call-and-answer promise (v.3) is followed by healing imagery: God will "heal" Jerusalem, "let them enjoy abundant peace and security" (v.6), and "cleanse them from all the sin they have committed" (v.8). The sounds of joy -- gladness, bride and bridegroom, thanksgiving -- will return to the desolate streets (v.11).

sections.header: Verses 14-26 -- The Branch and the Davidic Covenant

heb.paragraph: God will cause "a righteous Branch (tsemakh tsedaqa) to sprout from David's line" (v.15). This rephrases 23:5-6 with the addition of a double covenant guarantee: the Davidic dynasty and the Levitical priesthood will endure as long as the natural order (vv.20-21). The Branch is not merely a king but a royal-priestly figure.

refs.note: The Davidic covenant -- "your house and your kingdom will endure forever." Jeremiah reaffirms this promise during the dynasty's apparent collapse.
Jesus as priest in the order of Melchizedek fulfills both the Davidic (royal) and Levitical (priestly) strands that Jeremiah holds together.

notes.note: The Branch oracle repeated from 23:5-6, now with "Jerusalem" bearing the name "The LORD Our Righteousness" -- the city, not just the king, embodies divine righteousness.
Two permanent institutions: David's throne and the Levitical priesthood. Both are guaranteed by divine covenant, not human performance.
The creation covenant (day and night) as guarantor: if you can break the cycle of day and night, then you can break God's covenant with David. The argument from impossibility.

Calvin celebrates the creation-covenant parallel: "Every sunrise is a sermon on the reliability of God's promises. Nature itself testifies that the God who keeps day and night will keep his covenant with David."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: These verses are absent from the LXX of Jeremiah, which is generally shorter than the MT. Some scholars consider them a later expansion; others argue the LXX shortened an original longer text.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom notes that the creation-covenant argument (vv.20-26) extends the logic of 31:35-37: the permanence of the cosmos guarantees the permanence of God's people and God's messianic purpose.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the creation-covenant argument as "the most audacious theological claim in the book." At the moment when the Davidic dynasty is about to end, the prophet declares it more permanent than the stars.

hist.context: Verses 14-26 are absent from the LXX, suggesting they may be a later addition to the Hebrew text. Regardless of composition history, they serve a clear theological function: anchoring the messianic promise in creation theology. Just as God's covenant with day and night cannot be broken (v.20), so his covenant with David and the Levites cannot be broken. The permanence of the natural order guarantees the permanence of the messianic promise.

rows.label: vv.10-13
rows.text: Joy Returns to the Desolate Land

lit.note: Chapter 33 pairs prayer promise (v.3) with covenant guarantee (vv.19-26). The Branch oracle and creation-covenant argument provide the theological anchor for restoration hope.

themes.note: Covenant peaks at 10 -- creation itself guarantees the Davidic covenant. Mercy at 9 with comprehensive restoration.

hebtext.note: "Call to me and I will answer you and tell you great and unsearchable things (betsurот) you do not know" (v.3). The root btsr means "to fortify / make inaccessible." God promises to reveal things that are fortified against human discovery -- truths that cannot be reached by human effort but only by divine disclosure. This is revelation theology: truth comes from God, not from human investigation.

God will cause "a righteous Branch (tsemakh tsedaqa) to sprout from David's line" (v.15). This rephrases 23:5-6 with the addition of a double covenant guarantee: the Davidic dynasty and the Levitical priesthood will endure as long as the natural order (vv.20-21). The Branch is not merely a king but a royal-priestly figure.

ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: Chapter 33 continues the consolation while Jeremiah is still confined in the courtyard of the guard…

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: 20), so his covenant with David and the Levites cannot be broken…

translations.text: in the fight with the Babylonians: ‘They will be filled with the dead bodies of the people I will slay in my anger and wrath. I will hide my face from this city because of all its wickedness.

They are coming in to fight against the Chaldeans and to fill them with the dead bodies of men whom I shall strike down in my anger and my wrath, for I have hidden my face from this city because of all their evil.

Then this city will bring me renown, joy, praise and honor before all nations on earth that hear of all the good things I do for it; and they will be in awe and will tremble at the abundant prosperity and peace I provide for it.’

And this city shall be to me a name of joy, a praise and a glory before all the nations of the earth who shall hear of all the good that I do for them. They shall fear and tremble because of all the good and all the prosperity I provide for it.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: ""Call to me" -- the invitation comes while Jeremiah is imprisoned. God's revelation is not limited by the prophet's circumstances. Prison walls do not block prayer."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin calls this "the charter of prayer: God binds himself to answer. The promise is not conditional on the worthiness of the pray-er but on the faithfulness of the One who promises.""

rec.quote: "The word betsurот (often translated "unsearchable" or "hidden") literally means "fortified/inaccessible." The imagery is of truth locked in a fortress that only God can open."

debate: These verses are absent from the LXX of Jeremiah, which is generally shorter than the MT. Some scholars consider them a later expansion; others argue …
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Restoration Promises within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: "You will call upon me and come and pray to me, and I will listen" -- the same call-response dynamic from the letter to the exiles.
"If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God" -- the NT invitation to seek divine revelation through prayer.
The Davidic covenant -- "your house and your kingdom will endure forever." Jeremiah reaffirms this promise during the dynasty's apparent collapse.
Jesus as priest in the order of Melchizedek fulfills both the Davidic (royal) and Levitical (priestly) strands that Jeremiah holds together.

coaching.tip: 'Call to me and I will answer you and tell you great and unsearchable things you do not know' (v. 3). This invitation comes while Jeremiah is confined in the courtyard of the guard — imprisoned. The greatest revelations come during the deepest confinement. Paul writes Ephesians from prison; John receives Revelation on Patmos. Restricted circumstances don't restrict God's communication.

The 'righteous Branch' reappears (v. 15), linking back to 23:5. But now Jeremiah adds: 'David will never fail to have a man to sit on the throne' (v. 17). The Davidic covenant is declared permanent even as the dynasty is being destroyed. Jeremiah holds two contradictory realities simultaneously: the throne is falling and the throne is forever. Only messianic fulfillment resolves the paradox.
